name: 3° AV Block (Complete Heart Block)
identifyBy: Complete dissociation of P waves and QRS complexes - no relationship
P waves march through at their own rate (60-100 bpm)
QRS complexes fire at a slow escape rate (20-60 bpm)
Narrow QRS escape if junctional origin (40-60 bpm)
Wide QRS escape if ventricular origin (<40 bpm, less reliable)

measurements.prInterval: No fixed relationship & varies beat to beat
measurements.qrsDuration: Narrow if junctional; Wide if ventricular escape

management: Assess haemodynamic stability (BP, mental state, chest pain)
Atropine 0.6mg IV may speed junctional escape (ineffective for ventricular escape)
Transcutaneous pacing if haemodynamically unstable (hypotension, altered consciousness)
Do NOT rely on atropine alone if wide-complex escape
pearls: Narrow-complex CHB (junctional escape) is more stable than wide-complex (ventricular escape)
Inferior STEMI is a common cause - AV node ischaemia (transient, usually recovers)
Anterior STEMI causing CHB indicates extensive damage and has a poor prognosis
Ventriculophasic sinus arrhythmia: in CHB, P-P intervals flanking a QRS are shorter and confirms complete dissociation
Atropine works only on the AV node and ventricular escape rhythms (wide QRS) do not respond
differentials: 2° AV Block Mobitz II (not complete as some P waves conduct)
AV dissociation (isorhythmic has a different mechanism, different rates)
